summary: Kenneth Suna analyzes high-yield covered call ETFs (MSTY, etc.) claiming 100%+ dividend yields, exposing them as value traps where declining underlying stock prices offset premium income and lead to net underperformance versus holding the underlying stock directly.
takeaways: YieldMax MSTY paid average 111% dividend over 12 months but fund value dropped 35%, resulting in 55% total return vs. 126% gain from holding MSTR stock directly
Covered call ETFs underperform when underlying stock volatility normalizes because lower volatility = lower option premiums = fund value declines and dividend payments decrease
MSTY (139% yield) down 35% in 12 months while MSTR stock up 126% in same period demonstrates the drag of capped upside in covered call structure
High fees and tax inefficiency in covered call ETFs add hidden cost vs. managing covered calls manually
Manual covered call strategy requires $16,500 minimum to sell one contract (100 shares), making small accounts ineligible for premium generation
These funds work only for investors under $16,500 who cannot afford 100 shares to sell covered calls manually, but this is a poor long-term value trap
title: 100% dividend?! Too good to be true??
